coredata.dc:description: This work reports on the preparation of N, S-CQDs from melon seed shell, a common household waste that can inhibit the corrosion of copper in a 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. Additionally, by varying the preparation time and preparation temperature, the effects of N, S-CQDs on the capacity of copper to resist corrosion in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution were compared. It was also used to prove its inhibitory effect on copper in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution by a series of test methods. The corrosion inhibition rate of N, S-CQDs was 99.66 % and 99.87 % when the N, S-CQDs prepared at 160 ℃ for 6 h and 200 ℃ for 10 h were added. The results indicate that the corrosion inhibition rate of N, S-CQDs increases with increasing preparation time and temperature. XPS (X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy) and ATR-FTIR (Fourier transform infrared) were used to describe the composition and functional groups adsorbed on the surface of copper. N, S-CQDs' corrosion inhibition property was demonstrated by using an AFM (Atomic force microscope) and SEM (Scanning electron microscopy) to examine the corrosion surface morphology. Using the Langmuir adsorption model, it was found that the corrosion of copper was inhibited by both physical and chemical adsorption. A number of experimental findings demonstrate that preparation temperature and time impact the degree of carbonization, size, and functional group content of the N, S-CQDs, which in turn influences the corrosion inhibition of the N, S-CQDs. By means of heteroatom adsorption and surface structure, N, S-CQDs generate new chemical coordination bonds on the copper surface, thereby forming a protective layer, reducing the interaction between copper and 0.5 M H2SO4 solution, and generating a corrosion inhibition effect.

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: This work reports on the preparation of N, S-CQDs from melon seed shell, a common household waste that can inhibit the corrosion of copper in a 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. Additionally, by varying the preparation time and preparation temperature, the effects of N, S-CQDs on the capacity of copper to resist corrosion in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution were compared. It was also used to prove its inhibitory effect on copper in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution by a series of test methods. The corrosion inhibition rate of N, S-CQDs was 99.66 % and 99.87 % when the N, S-CQDs prepared at 160 ℃ for 6 h and 200 ℃ for 10 h were added. The results indicate that the corrosion inhibition rate of N, S-CQDs increases with increasing preparation time and temperature. XPS (X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy) and ATR-FTIR (Fourier transform infrared) were used to describe the composition and functional groups adsorbed on the surface of copper. N, S-CQDs' corrosion inhibition property was demonstrated by using an AFM (Atomic force microscope) and SEM (Scanning electron microscopy) to examine the corrosion surface morphology. Using the Langmuir adsorption model, it was found that the corrosion of copper was inhibited by both physical and chemical adsorption. A number of experimental findings demonstrate that preparation temperature and time impact the degree of carbonization, size, and functional group content of the N, S-CQDs, which in turn influences the corrosion inhibition of the N, S-CQDs. By means of heteroatom adsorption and surface structure, N, S-CQDs generate new chemical coordination bonds on the copper surface, thereby forming a protective layer, reducing the interaction between copper and 0.5 M H2SO4 solution, and generating a corrosion inhibition effect.

ce:section.ce:para: Copper is one of the oldest metals ever excavated, it has excellent thermal conductivity, malleability, electrical conductivity and chemical stability [1–3]. Copper was first utilized in the production of weapons and appliances, and humans also used copper coins as money. These days, it is widely employed in many field, including the electrical, chemical, electronic, and architectural [4,5]. Nevertheless, copper is prone to corrosion while in use, which shortens the equipment's lifespan and wastes a significant amount of money and resources [6]. In the realm of metal corrosion protection, pickling is usually employed to eliminate the oxide layer and corrosion products from the metal surface. Because diluted sulfuric acid won't produce copper base corrosion in an anaerobic environment, sulfuric acid is frequently employed as one of the copper lotions in the pickling process, but pickling process, dilute sulfuric acid often dissolve oxygen in the air and cause the corrosion of copper base, so adding corrosion inhibitor can prevent copper excessive corrosion, greatly improve the service cycle of copper and reduce the waste of acid [7,8].

It is commonly recognized that there are two types of corrosion inhibitors: organic and inorganic [9–12]. In recent times, 0.5 M H2SO4 solution has frequently employed organic molecules as copper inhibitors. Numerous studies have demonstrated that heterocycles, such as Nitrogen, Sulfur, Oxygen, Phosphorus, etc [13–18], are typically present in organic compounds, and that these heterocycles may readily bind to the metal surface to create protective layers [19–28]. Numerous copper corrosion inhibitors have been identified to far, such as imidazole derivatives [29], quinoline derivatives [30], thiophene derivatives [31], etc. Green chemistry's goals are undermined by the overuse of these inhibitors, which contaminates the environment. Therefore, it is urgent to develop a green pollution-free corrosion inhibitor. In recent years, plant extract has gained popularity as a metal preservative, such as mangifera leaves [32], mango pulp [33], nettles leaves [10], lychee [34], bodhi tree [35], etc. In addition, the extraction of corrosion inhibitors from biomass waste and is of great significance to the recycling and garbage classification in China [36,37].

CQDs (carbon quantum dots) as a hot concern in materials in recent years, are widely used in cell imaging, fluorescence sensing, drug delivery and particle detection. As a zero-dimensional carbon nanomaterials, it has a strong edge effect and quantum constraint effect [38]. At present, the preparation methods of CQDs are bottom-up method and top-down method [39]. The bottom-up method includes template method, microwave method, reverse micelle method, and hydrothermal method; the top-down method includes chemical oxidation, arc discharge, chemical ablation, and laser ablation. Compared with the bottom-up method and top-down, the carbon spots' size and shape can be more effectively controlled using the bottom-up approach, and the top-down approach requires demanding experimental conditions, expensive equipment, and difficult control of size and carbon spot morphology [40,41]. The preparation of carbon points by hydrothermal method is an ideal method because of its simple preparation process and uniform particle size [42].

In this work, we employed the common household waste of melon seed shells as presoma, added thiourea synthesized N, S-CQDs with good corrosion prevention capabilities by hydrothermal them. Compared with previous reports, Li et al. [43] created 4 ∼ 8 nm sized fluorescent corrosion inhibitors from disodium EDTA, urotropine salt and sulfamic acid; they were then employed to investigate how these inhibitors behaved while preventing N80 steel from corroding in a 3.5 wt% NaCl solution. These N, S-CQDs prepared by traditional organic chemical reagents have problems such as high toxicity and high price. Zhang et al. [44] synthesized N, S-CQDs using biomass soot as carbon source, Jing et al. [45] synthesized N, S-CQDs using coke with high efficiency, and Mahat et al. [46] transformed oil palm biomass using low-temperature hydrothermal methods and carbonization activation to produce optical N, S-CQDs. As a renewable natural resource, biomass is composed of carbon, hydrogen, oxygen, nitrogen and sulfur, among which carbon and oxygen account for the largest proportion, after carbonization treatment, 85 % of the material produced is still carbon [47]. In a word, biomass or its waste has the advantages of safety and biocompatibility. Therefore, it is safe, reliable and green to prepare CQDs as corrosion inhibitor from biomass. As one of the common snacks in people's life, sunflower seeds produce a large number of melon seed shells across the country every day, and it is a good idea to effectively use the sunflower seed shells for industrial development. In addition, the extraction of corrosion inhibitors from biomass waste and is of great significance to the recycling and garbage classification in China. Most importantly, there are no reports of corrosion inhibitor extraction from the common household waste melon seed shells to inhibitor to lessen copper corrosion in sulfuric acid. Besides, it has been shown that impurity-doped CQDs can alter their surface structure, expand their reaction site, and boost their quantum yield in addition to these effects. This is because their surface is covered in several functional groups that enable them to disperse widely in water [48,49]. Therefore, in this paper, N, S-CQDs were synthesized hydrothermally by using melon seed shell and thiourea, and its inhibitory effect on the copper corrosion behavior in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution was also studied. The effects of preparation conditions on the corrosion inhibition of N, S-CQDs were also investigated. N, S-CQDs produced at various temperatures and time exhibit a good inhibitory effect on copper corrosion in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution, according to experiments.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Synthesis of N, S-CQDs and solution preparation
ce:section.ce:para: The discarded melon seed shells were fully crushed in the shredder, and the melon seeds were purchased in an ordinary supermarket in Chongqing University City. The products are authentic fragrant melon seeds, with ingredients including raw melon seeds and a small amount of salt. Dissolve 3.0 g of melon seed shell powder and 3.0 g of thiourea in about 60 mL of hyperpure water, and transfer the dissolved solution to 100 mL of polytetrafluoroethylene-lined autoclave at 200 ℃ for 10 h. After its natural cooling to get the red brown suspension, through the funnel initial filter to remove large particles, then 0.22 μm needle filter to remove smaller impurity particles, then transfer all the resulting solution to the dialysis bag (retention amount ∼ 1000 Da), the dialysis bag into the hyperpure water for 24 h to remove unreacted particles, during this period, the hyperpure water was changed every 6 h, and then all the liquid in the dialysis bag was transferred to the beaker into the refrigerator for 12 h and dried in the freeze drying box, the brown solid is N, S-CQDs. To explore the optimal conditions for preparing N, S-CQDs, the preparation temperatures were changed to 160 ℃, 180 ℃ and 200 ℃, and the preparation times were 6 h, 8 h and 10 h, respectively. Subsequent purification procedure is the same as above. The resulting carbon dots prepared at 160 ℃ 6 h are denoted as N, S-CQDs-1 and those prepared at 200 ℃ 10 h as N, S-CQDs-2. The specific schematic diagram is shown in Fig. 1 .

98 % concentrated sulfuric acid was diluted to 0.5 M as a blank control solution. The experimental solution was a 0.5 M H2SO4 solution of N, S-CQDs supplemented contain or without different preparation conditions and different concentrations.

A 1 cm3 copper block and a copper wire with a surface length of 10 cm were connected to create the working electrode, which was then covered with epoxy resin to reserve a 1 cm2 working area. The working electrodes were polished to a flat surface using sandpaper grades 400, 800, 1200 and 2000 prior to testing. Following a cleaning with hyperpure water and absolute ethanol, the working electrode's surface was dried by a cold wind.

Before testing, polish the 0.5 × 0.5 × 0.5 cm3 copper sample and 1 × 1 × 1 cm3 copper. The copper material is successively ground on 400, 800, 1200, 2000, 3000, 5000, 7000 mesh sandpaper until the surface is smooth and translucent, and dry them with cold air. Among them, the 0.5 × 0.5 × 0.5 cm3 copper is used for scanning electron microscopy (SEM), and 1 × 1 × 1 cm3 copper is used for atomic force microscopy (AFM）, fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (ATR-FTIR) and X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS). The copper sample was submerged in the 0.5 M H2SO4 solution containing 200 mg/L N, S-CQDs or without it prior to testing. The elements and functional groups adsorbed on the copper surface were found using XPS (PHI-5700) and ATR-FTIR (Nicolet-iS50). Furthermore, the AFM (MFP-3D-BIO) and the SEM (S-4800) was utilized to identify the metal surface's roughness and examine the corrosion morphology, respectively.

The adsorption process features of N, S-CQDs and copper can be better understood by examining changes in the open circuit potential (E ocp). The EIS test must also be performed at the open circuit point stability. The OCP-time curve of copper in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution with or without N, S-CQDs prepare under varying conditions, is displayed in Fig. 2 (a, c, e, g). In comparison to the blank solution, all Eocp curves showed a considerable negative shift, with Eocp moving in a more negative direction as concentration increased. Notably, the solution containing N, S-CQDs displayed a declining trend in the first 600 s compared to the blank solution, which might be connected to the dissolution of copper, and then gradually stabilized thereafter, indicating that the desorption and adsorption on the working electrode reach a dynamic equilibrium [50], additionally, it demonstrates that N, S-CQDs have a good corrosion inhibitors action, generating a thick protective film layer on the material surface to stop copper from further corroding [33]. Fig. 2 (e, h) also shows that with the same amount of addition, as the preparation temperature increases and the time becomes longer, Eocp gradually moves more negative.

Fig. 2 (b, d, f, h) illustrates the tafel curves in various conditions. The shape of the tafel curve of N, S-CQDs prepared under different hydrothermal condition is roughly unchanged, indicating that the change of preparation conditions did not change the inhibition mechanism of copper by N, S-CQDs. Compared with the 0.5 M H2SO4 solution, after the addition of the two different N, S-CQDs, the anode and cathode electrodes of the tafel curve move towards a lower potential direction, and the cathode moves more significantly. The results indicate that N, S-CQDs mainly affect the cathodic reaction, and the corrosion potential decreases with the increase of the amount of N, S-CQDs, which is a significant characteristic of the cathodic corrosion inhibitor [51]. It is noteworthy that after adding N, S-CQDs, the tafel curve of anode branch appeared a plateau, and with the increase of N, S-CQDs concentration, the plateau curve gradually elongated, which may be due to the fact that N, S-CQDs nanomolecules adsorbed on the surface of copper electrode formed a dense protective film, resulting in the passivation of the anode branch [52].

By fitting the polarization curve near the corrosion potential using classical extrapolation, we determined the icorr (corrosion current density), Ecorr (corrosion potential), βc and βa (cathodic and anodic slopes of the polarization curve parameters) as shown in Table 1 . To compute the corrosion inhibition efficiency (η) use this formula (1) [53–65]:

Specifically, icorr, 0 and icorr are represented in the sulfuric acid solution with or without N, S-CQDs, respectively. Table 1 shows that the icorr steadily drops and that the Ecorr is dramatically shifted negatively. The degree of corrosion of the metal is represented by the icorr; the lower the icorr , the better the metal's protection and suppressing function [66]. Therefore, the addition of N, S-CQDs reduces the icorr , proves that it attenuates the corrosion behavior of copper in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution, and as the amount added increases, the corrosion inhibition effect becomes more pronounced that the corrosion inhibition effect can reach up to 95.9 % when the addition amount of N, S-CQDs-2 is 200 mg/L. Table 1 showed that compared with the 0.5 M H2SO4 solution, the Ecorr of the solution containing N, S-CQDs significantly shifted by more than 85 mV, except for the Ecorr change of 53 mV at a preparation condition of 160 ℃ for 6 h. According to the literature, the inhibitor is either an anode or a cathode inhibitor if the difference in the Ecorr between a solution containing the inhibitor and blank solution exceeds 85 mV; otherwise, it is a mixture inhibitor [51]. It shows that N, S-CQDs are mixed inhibitors with short preparation time (6 h), low preparation temperature (160 ℃) and low addition concentration (10 mg/L), and mainly affect the cathode; and the remaining conditions are cathodic inhibitors. It also indicates that N, S-CQDs have good adsorption and inhibition ability. According to literature, the following is the mechanism of copper corrosion in the sulfuric solution [67,68]:

Cathode reaction:

Through formula (2, 3), we can know that formula (4) is a quick decisive step. The primary function of the cathodic reaction is to extract electrons from dissolved oxygen and reduce them to OH−, which combines with H+ in a solution of sulfuric acid to generate water. And the anode reaction involves two processes, the first step being the loss of electrons in copper and the transformation into Cu+, while the second step is for Cu+ to lose another ion and transform into Cu2+ [69,70].

As shown in Table 1, when adding the N, S-CQDs, βc falls, suggesting a change in the cathode's process. The reason for this is that the adsorption of the inhibitor on the copper substrate mainly influences the diffusion of O2 into the cathode of the copper electrode in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution [71]. It is worth noting that by comparing the parameters of different preparation times and temperatures, it is unclear if modifications to the preparation settings will have an impact on how the cathode and anode's reaction mechanisms alter. However, relevant literature suggests that longer preparation times can change the size of carbon dots and the amount of hydrophilic groups with oxygen, like OH– and COOH–, thereby reducing the hydrophilic groups at the copper/sulfuric acid interface and achieving corrosion inhibition [72–77]. However, it cannot be proven that the preparation temperature directly affects the size of carbon dots, but the level of preparation temperature can change the degree of biomass carbonization [73,78]. Therefore, the preparation temperature may affect the quantum efficiency of N, S-CQDs by affecting the degree of carbonization of melon seed shells, thereby achieving a corrosion inhibition [79]. Even so, the N, S-CD prepared at a lower temperature and in a short time still has good corrosion inhibition performance for copper, and the corrosion inhibition efficiency can reach 76.83 % in the sulfuric acid solution only with 10 mg/L N, S-CQDs-1.

The EIS test aims to study the corrosion mechanism of copper in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. Fig. 3 shows the Nyquist diagram and the Bode diagram in the 0.5 M H2SO4 solution of copper with or without N, S-CQDs under different preparation conditions. According to Fig. 3(a, c, e, g), the impedance spectrum shows a depressed semi round arc in the high-frequency region, and a straight line in the low-frequency area which may be cause by the Warburg impedance created by dissolved oxygen in solution [80]. Because Cu has a higher potential than hydrogen, the anodic reaction causes copper to dissolve, which leads to diffusion resistance, and the cathode produces hydrogen precipitation and oxygen reduction reaction. All the same time, the radius of the capacitance ring increases with the growth the doping amount of N, S-CQDs. The radius of the capacitance ring represents the impedance value of copper, that is, the corrosion resistance of copper, indicating that N, S-CQDs form a shielding film on the material surface, increasing the resistance, so that the copper matrix is less easy to corrosion. The shape curve after the addition of N, S-CQDs was roughly unchanged from the blank solution curve, which indicated that the addition of N, S-CQDs did not change their electrochemical reaction. However, after adding N, S-CQDs, the Warburg effect on copper matrix disappears, indicating that N, S-CQDs can form a protective film on material matrix to prevent direct contact between copper matrix and copper matrix [81,82], thus realizing the corrosion inhibition of copper matrix. In Fig. 3 (b, d, f, h) Bode diagram, compared with the blank solution, the impedance mode value increases significantly, and more obviously with the concentration of N, S-CQDs. In addition, the slope of log | Z | and log f is close to 1 in a wide frequency range, which shows that the copper electrode surface has ideal capacitive behavior. As the concentration of N, S-CQDs increases, the phase diagram becomes significantly wider. This is probably due to the overlapping of two peaks in the phase diagram, indicating the presence of two time constants, one due to the relaxation of the double-layer capacitance and the other due to the desorption of N, S-quantum dots on the copper surface [52].

The EIS data is fitted into the equivalent circuit shown in Fig. 4 ; the resultant fitted data is shown in Table 2 , the corrosion reaction's charge transfer resistance is denoted by Rct , the corrosion product film's membrane resistance by Rf , the corrosion solution resistance is represented by Rs , and the diffusion-induced Warburg resistance is denoted by W. The double-layer capacitor relevant to the constant phase angle element is called CPEdl , and the corrosion product film's capacitive response is represented by CPEf . The formula (5) can be used to determine the CPE's impedance [19,83–94]:

where Rp,0 , and Rp stand for the total charge Rct and R f, respectively, with and without N, S-CQDs.

From Table 2, it can be seen that η gradually increases with increasing concentrations of Rct , probably due to the fact that N, S-CQDs, which are adsorbed on the material electrode surface, hinder the transfer of charge and inhibit the corrosion of the copper electrode. With the rises of concentration of N, S-CQDs, this hindrance will be more and more strong, therefore, N, S-CQDs corrosion inhibition will be better. Moreover, Cf and Cdl gradually decreased with the increasing concentration, indicating that this may be due to the gradual substitution of H2O and SO4 2- molecules by N, S-CQDs at the material/solution interface to organize the further corrosion of the material interface; the dielectric constant drops and the electrode's active area shrinks as concentration rises [103]. It was found that the η value increased with the addition amount, when the preparation temperature is 200 ℃ and the preparation time is 10 h, 200 mg/L N, S-CQDs was added, and the η was equal to 99.87 %.

The ATR-FTIR of copper immersed in 0.5 M H2SO4 solutions containing N, S-CQDs-1 and N, S-CQDs-2 are shown in Fig. 5 . The large broad peak at 3380 cm−1 is the characteristic stretching vibration peak of O–H or N–H [104]; the faint peak at 2120 cm−1 may be caused by C = N = S [105]; the peak at 1640 cm−1 is attributed to C = C, C = N and C = O [106]; the 1160 cm−1 is the telescopic vibration of C–O–C and C–O [103]; the peak at 1050 cm−1 may be C–SO3 [107] ; and the peak at 877 cm−1 and 588 cm−1 is attributed to the telescopic vibration of C–H. In addition, the O–H and N–H intensities decreased significantly in the infrared spectra of N, S-CQDs prepared at 200 °C for 10 h. Therefore, the results of ATR-FTIR spectroscopy show that there are many functional groups (C = C, N–H, O–H C = C, C = O, etc.) on the surface of copper, and as temperature and time increase, the content of hydrophilic groups like N–H and O–H decreases, supporting the electrochemical results and demonstrating the adsorptive N, S-CQDs on copper surfaces.

The XPS test delves further into the adsorption bonding between N, S-CQDs and the copper surface. In a 298 K continuous water bath in H2SO4 solution with or without 200 mg/L N, S-CQDs-2, copper was submerged for 36 h. The entire XPS spectrum is displayed in Fig. 6 (a, b). Further evidence that N, S-CQDs are adsorbed on the copper sheet comes from the observation that the peak of the N element is clearly visible in the copper piece soaked in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution containing N, S-CQDs-2 when compared to the copper sheet soaked in the blank 0.5 M H2SO4 solution. By comparing the amounts of each element in the two spectra, we can observe that the C and N elements increase, showing that N, S-CQDs already adsorb on the material sheet surface. However, the influence of sulfate makes it impossible to determine the amounts of the S and O elements. On the other hand, the Cu element concentration is lower, which can be because to N, S-CQDs adhering to the copper surface and lowering the exposed Cu area. Comparing Fig. 6 (c, d), typical Cu 2p bimodal peaks can be observed at 932.63 eV and 952.38 eV, corresponding to Cu 2p3/2/Cu (I) and Cu 2p1/2/Cu (I) peaks, respectively, attributed to the production of copper substrates and copper ions. Accompanying peaks of Cu 2p3/2 (II) and Cu 2p1/2 (II) appeared at 934.93 eV and 954.43 eV between Cu 2p3/2/Cu (I) and Cu2p1/2 (II) [33], but compared to Fig. 6 (c, d), Cu (II) decreased significantly, proving that N, S-CQDs can slow down the transition of Cu(I) to Cu (II), but Cu (II) still exists. Fig. 6 (e) shows three peaks, which stand for C–C/C = C (284.83 eV), C = O (285.58 eV), and C-O-C (288.43 eV) [108], in that order. The C–C/C = C, C = O, and C = N peaks were found at 284.83 eV, 286.13 eV, and 287.18 eV in Fig. 6 (f). The addition of C = N confirms that N, S-CQDs are adsorbed on the surface of the copper substrate. Observing Fig. 6 (g, h), the corresponding peaks of CuO/Cu2O all appeared at the 531.68 eV, but a peak of C-O-C was observed at 533.13 eV in Fig. 6 (h), proving the adsorption of N, S-CQDs on the copper surface. The comparison between the newly added S-Cu at 163.08 eV in Fig. 6 (i, j) and the N-Cu peak at 400.33 eV in Fig. 6 (k) that N, S-CQDs are adsorbed on the surface of the material substrate by forming new bonds.

Analysis of adsorption mechanism of N, S-CQDs on metal surface by Langmuir adsorption model. The Langmuir adsorption model expression is provided in formula (7) [20,109–112]:

Generally, the spontaneity and type of adsorption behavior of the N, S-CQDs molecules are determined the ΔG0 ads . If the ΔG0 ads value is less than −40 kJ/mol, it is thought to be a chemical adsorption produced by chemical bonding; if it is larger than −20 kJ/mol, it is thought to be the active molecules are adsorbed on the metal substrate by means of electrostatic adsorption [116]. As can be seen from Fig. 7 , ΔG0 ads are less than 0, which indicates that the adsorption of N, S-CQDs on the copper surface is spontaneous. As seen, with prepared at 160 ℃, 6 h and 200 ℃, 10 h, the ΔG0 ads in the sulfuric acid solution of N, S-CQDs were −31.04 kJ/moL and −34.37 kJ/moL respectively, and the absolute value of Gads is between 20 and 40 kJ / mol, this proves that there is both chemisorption and physical adsorption on the copper surface.

The SEM image of the copper electrode immersed in 0.5 M sulfuric acid solution with or without 200 mg/L N, S-CQDs for 36 h is shown in Fig. 8 . In Fig. 8 (a), there are many corrosion marks on the material surface and obvious corrosion pits after soaking by the sulfuric acid blank solution, indicating that the copper sheet has suffered severe corrosion. The surface of Fig. 8 (b,c) is relatively flat, and the sandpaper-polished scratches are still clearly visible, with only a few corrosion pits appearing. It is worth noting that compared with Fig. 8 (b) and Fig.(c), the copper soaked in N, S-CQDs-1 have more traces of corrosion than the copper soaked in N, S-CQDs-2, which indicates that N, S-CQDs-2 have better corrosion inhibition, which is consistent with the electrochemical test results.

AFM can directly observe the surface morphology of metal materials, thus it is widely used in surface research of corrosion inhibition materials. The material was soaked in sulfuric acid solution with or without 200 mg/L N, S-CQDs-1 or N, S-CQDs-2 for 36 h. In Fig. 9 (a), the freshly polished copper surface is flat, smooth, and has a roughness of just 11.1. As can be seen in Fig. 9 (b), the material surface is uneven, with severe depressions and protrusions, which indicates that the copper surface is severely corroded, and the roughness can reach 58.6. However, in Fig. 9 (c, d) shows that the copper surface is relatively flat, and the roughness drops to 24.9 and 16.3 respectively. Compared with the roughness and surface topography of copper immersed in 0.5 M H2SO4 solution containing two different N, S-CQDs for 36 h, we can see that the corrosion inhibition effect of N, S-CQDs-2 is stronger than N, S-CQDs-1, which is consistent with the electrochemical and SEM results. It is further confirmed by the experimental results that N, S-CQDs can effectively prevent corrosion in the sulfuric acid. The N, S-CQDs that are prepared at a high temperature (200 °C) and for a longer period of time (10 h) have a stronger corrosion suppression performance than those that are prepared at a low temperature (160 °C) and for a shorter period of time (6 h).

Fig. 10 illustrates the process by which N, S-CQDs limit copper corrosion in sulfuric acid based on experimental results. On the one hand, the electrostatic interaction of N, S-CQDs is used to physically adsorb them with copper-based materials; in addition, The heteroatoms of N, S-CQDs, including sulfur and nitrogen, are abundant on the surface and have the capacity to attract electrons, while the outer orbit of copper is easy to supply electrons. Therefore, nitrogen, sulfur and other heteroatoms can form a stable coordination bond with copper, and form a protective film on material surface, so as to decrease the direct contact of Cu substrate with corrosive medium. In summary, N, S-CQDs function as a corrosion inhibition agent by reducing the reaction active site of the cathode and anode in contact with sulfuric acid solution by forming a protective coating by a combination of physical and chemical adsorption.

In this article, N, S-CQDs have excellent anti-corrosion effect on copper in 0.5 M sulfuric acid solution. Changing the hydrothermal conditions (temperature and time) of the preparation can affect the corrosion inhibition effect of copper, and the corrosion inhibition efficiency is still above 95 % at a relatively low temperature (160 °C) and a short time (6 h) when the addition amount is 200 mg/L. The electrochemical results show that N, S-CQDs control the corrosion rate by controlling the charge transfer between the electrode and the solution. The results of scanning electron microscopy and AFM showed that the surface of the copper sheet soaked in different N, S-CQDS (N, S-CQDS-1 and N, S-CQDs-2) sulfuric acid solutions was still relatively flat, and the scratches after sandpaper grinding were still clearly visible. The XPS results showed that N, S-CQDs could form new bonds (N-Cu, S-Cu) and adsorb on the copper surface to form a dense protective film to prevent copper corrosion. In addition, the adsorption of N, S-CQDs on the copper surface conforms to the Langmuir monolayer adsorption, which has physical and chemical adsorption.
